title: SEGMENT 13 — Catching it early: screening, vaccines, and why timing matters

lines.text: SEGMENT 13 — Catching it early: screening, vaccines, and why timing matters

Let me step back from drug mechanisms for a moment and talk about something that, statistically, has done more to reduce cancer deaths in the last fifty years than any individual drug: catching cancer early, or preventing it from happening at all.
The core idea is simple.
Remember our hallmarks story — cancer is a stack of capabilities acquired one mutation at a time.
Early cancers have acquired fewer of them.
Localized cancers haven't yet learned to metastasize.
The earlier in this progression you intervene, the easier the intervention.
The analogy: a small fire in a wastepaper basket is something you put out with a glass of water.
The same fire, given an hour to spread through a building, requires a fire department, and even then it may take the building.
Cancer is the same.
Early is everything.
There are two broad strategies for "early.
" One is screening — looking for cancers that have started but haven't caused symptoms yet.
The other is prevention — stopping cancers from starting at all.
Screening.
The principle is that for certain cancers, we can detect them in a pre-symptomatic state where they're highly curable.
The classic example is cervical cancer.
The Pap smear, invented by George Papanicolaou in the 1920s, lets us look at cells scraped from the cervix and identify abnormal ones years before they would become invasive cancer.
Cervical cancer used to be one of the leading causes of cancer death in women.
In countries with widespread Pap screening, mortality has plummeted.

Other major screening programs: mammography for breast cancer, colonoscopy for colon cancer (you find polyps, you snip them out during the same procedure, and the cancer never happens), low-dose CT scanning of the chest in long-term smokers for lung cancer, and the much more controversial PSA blood test for prostate cancer.

Each of these has tradeoffs — false positives, overtreatment of cancers that would never have caused harm, costs, anxiety — and each is the subject of long-running debate about exactly who should be screened and how often.
But the broad conclusion stands: for cancers where good screening exists and is offered, you save lives.
Prevention.
Here's where things get really powerful, because the best cancer therapy is preventing the cancer from ever existing.
Let me give you three categories.
First, modifiable risk factors.
Tobacco causes roughly twenty percent of all cancer deaths worldwide — and not just lung cancer, but cancers of the mouth, throat, esophagus, bladder, pancreas, kidney, cervix, and more.
Tobacco cessation is the single most impactful cancer-prevention intervention available.
Alcohol is also a significant risk factor for several cancers.
Obesity is increasingly recognized as a major cancer risk.
UV exposure causes most skin cancers.
Each of these is something an individual can change.
Second, vaccination against cancer-causing infections.
About fifteen percent of cancers worldwide are caused by infectious agents.

The big ones: human papillomavirus, HPV, which causes cervical cancer and several other cancers; hepatitis B and C, which cause liver cancer; Helicobacter pylori, which causes stomach cancer; Epstein-Barr virus and HTLV-1 for certain blood cancers; HIV for several cancers indirectly.
The HPV vaccine story is one of the great public-health triumphs of our century.

HPV is a sexually transmitted virus that infects cervical, anal, throat, and other epithelial surfaces, and certain strains drive nearly all cervical cancer and a large fraction of other anogenital and head-and-neck cancers.
A vaccine against the most carcinogenic HPV strains was approved in 2006.
Countries that have rolled it out widely in early adolescence — Australia, the UK, Sweden — are now seeing cervical cancer rates collapsing in vaccinated cohorts.
Cervical cancer is, plausibly, on course to be eliminated as a disease in some countries within the next few decades.
That is a cancer that we vaccinated away.
Hepatitis B vaccination, in countries that introduced it broadly in the 1980s and 90s, is dramatically reducing liver cancer rates in those cohorts now.
Third, chemoprevention — using drugs to prevent cancer in high-risk individuals.
The standard example is tamoxifen, which can substantially reduce breast cancer risk in women at very high genetic risk.
PrEP-like long-term preventive cancer drugs for the general population remain mostly a research aspiration.
The deeper lesson: cancer therapy gets the headlines, but cancer prevention saves more lives, more cheaply, with less suffering.
A teenager who gets the HPV vaccine and never smokes has, statistically, eliminated a substantial fraction of their lifetime cancer risk before they've ever met an oncologist.
